These are going to be very simple explanations just to get you familiar with the terms. Okay, let's jump in right now. The first deal type I want to discuss is called the Leverage Buyout or LBO. Maybe you've heard of this term in some movie about Wall Street. All a leverage buyout means is you're acquiring a business using borrowed money. The term LBO alone doesn't tell you where that borrowed money came from and there are a lot of different sources as you'll see as we go through this program. But essentially an LBO is a common deal type to acquire a business with little to no money down. Here are the buyer and seller profiles for an LBO. First, a buyer looking for a good business with decent financials and regular profits that he or she likes and they prioritize paying little if any cash out of their own pocket to buy it, but instead use borrowed money. Second, a seller who has some reason to sell, maybe the seller's really tired. This has been super common with business owners during the stresses of COVID-19 pandemic. Or maybe the seller's interests have changed since they started the business. They're near or even past retirement age or maybe there was a death in the family. It could be any reason. This is where a buyer and seller meet and potentially the buyer can just leverage 100% of the purchase. This is a very simple explanation of a leveraged buyout. Okay, let's move on to the next deal type. The next type of deal I want to cover is a fire sale or distress sale. This involves buying a business that's in serious trouble. It's not insolvent yet, but it's cruising towards potential insolvency. If you're thinking, that sounds awful. Why would I want to buy a business that's potentially dying? Well, the answer is because it's in trouble. Oftentimes, it's in trouble because of the seller's mentality and mindset. Buyers can acquire these businesses very cheaply. And in certain situations, the business is salvageable. So especially if a new owner with a particular expertise or network or great vision or leadership skills comes in, adds value and turns it around. And if you can turn this business around with minor adjustments after buying it, creating unbelievable profitability, that's the point where you can then flip it and sell it for a quick profit. Just like flipping a house. Where you can keep it and continue to scale it and take passive income from it. The downside of this deal is that it will take hard work to turn the business around. Far more work than if you simply acquired a functioning, profitable, already growing business. Here are the buyer and seller profiles for a distress sale. First, a buyer with certain skills and who is up for a lot of hard work and risk to have a potentially big profit without having a large purchase price. Secondly, a seller who feels totally overwhelmed by the stress of their business struggling. They are close to just shutting it down. They view the buyer as a lifeline who will take this problem off their hands. The next deal type is a bolt-on acquisition. This is not a deal type someone acquiring a business for the first time would use, but it's still worth knowing. That's because you use this type of deal after you've already bought or owned a business. It's where you literally acquire a business specifically to bolt it onto your existing business platform. Typically, the bolt-on acquisition is a business that dovetails with your current business to increase efficiencies, increase effectiveness, and therefore profitability. Buying two complementary businesses can give you some synergy in running things and also gives you a lot of leverage in your industry. The buyer and seller profiles for a bolt-on acquisition look like this. For a buyer, it's buying an existing business that will complement their business to create more value. For a seller, it could be that they're weak in the marketplace and they don't have your infrastructure or the support to grow and scale. So they sell their service or product business to an existing business to create more value within that business. The next deal type you should know is what I call intrapreneurship deal. In fact, this is how I got started. This is where you join a business, either as a consultant, a key employee, whatever your position is, and you negotiate equity upside in the business as an employee, as a contributor. Then, since you have credibility with the business owner, your different vendors, maybe the banks, and you have knowledge of the business, you let the owner know that you would like to buy the business from them. The buyer and seller profiles for an intrapreneurship deal is this. In the buyer's case, this is someone who first joins a business and adds value, then expresses their intent to buy it from the owner. They receive funding to buy from investors, friends, family, or maybe even a supplier or customer after showing them proof of operational efficiency. They can even go to the bank, by the way, because they've been working with the bank, with the business owner. The more credibility you have, better chance you can buy the business. Now, for the seller, this is someone who may not even know they want to sell, but likes the idea of an intrapreneur who can come in and help them run the business, create value, take the pressure off of me as the business owner. I love intrapreneurs. For a seller, it creates a natural exit. The next deal type is called consulting for equity. It's similar to the intrapreneur in that the consultant is bringing value to the business, but in exchange for equity, not cash, they take equity for creating value. They might not end up owning the business, but instead a piece of it. The buyer and seller profiles for a consulting for equity deal is, in this case, the buyer offers their expertise on an ongoing basis in exchange for equity in the business versus cash. And usually, the seller is a business owner that's hit some kind of limitation in their business and needs help. And so therefore, they're willing to exchange equity instead of cash for the expertise and professionalism of the advisor. There's also the roll-up deal. This is where you acquire multiple small businesses in the same industry and bundle them together. It's called consolidation. The buyer and seller profiles for a roll-up deal is this. The buyer is someone who's looking to create massive value by acquiring as many competitors as possible and putting them into one company creating market dominance within the marketplace that they compete. For the sellers, these are owners who believe staying small and by themselves is probably not the best strategy. So they're willing to sell to a larger organization that has systems, has process, already has an operating team, professional finance team, the whole infrastructure required for massive scaling. In a roll-up strategy, buyers buy because they want to get bigger through acquiring competitors and adding them to their business. Seller sell because they're either too small, ready to retire, or they don't want to compete, so they sell to the bigger organization. Lastly, there's a broker deal. This is where you play middleman between a buyer and seller and you take equity in lieu of owning the business. To be honest with you, if you master this program, you can do a lot of broker deals as well. In the next modules, we'll take a closer look at three of the deals that will be most common.
